description:
Authors: Coda-Forno, J. and Binz, M. and Akata, Z. and Botvinick, M. and Wang, J. X. and Schulz, E.
Title: CogBench: Large language models reason about cognition
Year: 2024
URL: URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.09798
reason: The paper 'CogBench: Large language models reason about cognition' with the given authors and arXiv:2403.09798 does not exist. The actual CogBench paper has a different title, fewer authors (missing Akata and Botvinick), and a different arXiv ID (2402.18225). The provided arXiv ID belongs to a different paper entirely.
prompt_variant: You are a critical academic research verifier. Your task: determine if a bibliog...
_search_results:
**Yes, the paper exists.**

Exact title: **CogBench: a large language model walks into a psychology lab**[1][4][6][8][9][10].

Authors: **Julian Coda-Forno**<sup>1,2</sup>, **Marcel Binz**<sup>1,2</sup>, **Jane X. Wang**<sup>3</sup>, **Eric Schulz**<sup>1,2</sup> (note: Akata and Botvinick are not listed as authors; Schulz matches)[1][4][6].

Venue: arXiv preprint (presented or reviewed at ICML 2024)[4][9][10].

Year: **2024**.

arXiv: **2402.18225** (user-provided 2403.09798 does not match this paper; no results for that ID)[4][9].

PDF available at: https://hcai-munich.com/pubs/CodaForno2024CogBench.pdf[1] and https://arxiv.org/pdf/2402.18225[9].

**Abstract summary**: Introduces **CogBench**, a benchmark with **ten behavioral metrics** from **seven cognitive psychology experiments** to evaluate LLMs' cognitive behaviors (e.g., model size, RLHF effects on performance and human alignment). Applied to 35 LLMs using multilevel modeling[1][4][9].
Semantic Scholar: no matching paper found.

**No, the paper "CogBench: Large language models reason about cognition" with arXiv:2403.09798 does not exist.**

The arXiv ID 2403.09798 corresponds to a different paper titled **"Comparing Rationality Between Large Language Models and Humans"**, submitted on 14 March 2024, categorized under Computers and Society (cs.CY). Its abstract discusses Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) enhancing LLMs' rationality and decision-making, with a focus on comparisons to humans, particularly ChatGPT[7][6].

The queried title closely matches the actual **CogBench** paper, titled **"CogBench: a large language model walks into a psychology lab"** (arXiv:2402.18225v1 [cs.CL], submitted earlier in 2024). This introduces CogBench, a benchmark with **ten behavioral metrics** from **seven cognitive psychology experiments** to evaluate LLMs' cognitive behaviors, applied to 35 models using multilevel modeling. Key findings include RLHF and model size improving human-like alignment, open-source models being less risk-prone, and prompt techniques like chain-of-thought aiding probabilistic reasoning[2][1][8][4][10][9].

A unrelated later paper (arXiv:2508.03360, August 2025) uses the name **CogBench** for multilingual speech-based cognitive impairment assessment, not matching the query[3].

  Citations: 57

**Yes, the paper exists.**

**Exact title:** Comparing Rationality Between Large Language Models and Humans: Insights and Open Questions[3][4].

**Authors:** Dana Alsagheer, Rabimba Karanjai, Nour Diallo, Weidong Shi, Yang Lu, Suha Beydoun, Qiaoning Zhang[3][4].

**Venue:** arXiv (preprint)[3].

**Year:** 2024 (submitted 14 Mar 2024)[3][4].

**DOI:** https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2403.09798[3].

The paper examines rationality in LLMs (focusing on ChatGPT) versus humans using specific rationality tests, highlighting RLHF's role in improving LLM decision-making and noting LLMs' advantages like resistance to cognitive biases[2][3][4]. Humans showed better reasoning articulation in complex tasks despite lower scores[2][4]. A related later paper (arXiv:2406.17055v2) finds LLMs assume humans are **more rational** than they actually are[1].

_s2.title: CogBench: a large language model walks into a psychology lab
